Okay, well this next module is about email marketing. If you're not doing email marketing, you need to start because you're missing out on an enormous amount of sales on connecting with an enormous number of customers through email. Email is the way to grow your company. And to explain, one, why it's important and two, how to do it, we brought in Francis Jones. Francis Jones works with a company called Infusionsoft. And Infusionsoft is a massive company that helps over 30,000 businesses, usually mostly small businesses, right? Sub-5 million dollar businesses. It started in email marketing and you guys have an incredible platform that you can manipulate your email, you know when the email is going out. I mean, it's really fun to sort of map out your email campaign. It's just incredible stuff. But because you do that, you know what works and what doesn't work. So we thought for our customers, we want to go to the guy who knows what works in email marketing and what doesn't. So we brought in you. Great. First of all, why should we do... I mean, I'm telling everybody, right? But I want to hear from you. Why do they need to start doing email marketing? Email marketing is the lifeblood of any small business. As you know, you've not had conversations about this. If small businesses are not doing email marketing, they cannot grow. You just can't do it. You have to do something to try and nurture your audience and to bring them along. And if you're relying on them going to your websites or reading your assets, your materials, things like that, it's just not going to work. And it's not cheap, but it's a way that you can not have to do radio campaigns, not have to do television, not have to do that stuff. The reality is customers are walking around looking at their phones. And when you can get them an email and they click on that email once a week, maybe twice a month at least, that you're reminding them that you exist and you're reminding them that your products can solve their problems. And so if you're not doing that and your competitor starts doing it, they're going to box you out of the marketplace. Absolutely. All right. So if everybody's sold, right, we just learned how to write good email nerd emails and now we're going to start a campaign. We know how to create a lead generator from this course. We're going to learn how to write a great sales letter, all that kind of stuff. But talk to me about an email nurturing campaign. And let me explain kind of how we do business and probably one of the reasons you bought this course. You downloaded something, gave us your email, and we sent you a bunch of emails that sequentially on ramped you to buy this course or to buy something from Storybrand. That's what we're talking about. They downloaded the lead generator. Now you've got their email address. What do you do with that email address? You create a nurturing campaign. So explain to us how a nurturing campaign works. A nurturing campaign is designed to do just as you mentioned, is to educate the prospect. Very often people don't buy on initial impulse. You'll see it on your page views and your statistics and analytics. People drop off. And you want to provide a place and an opportunity for them to learn as they go along at their pace. The reality is people buy when they're ready to buy, not when you're ready to sell to them. Right. Right. And remember you when they're ready. Yeah, absolutely. You have to keep pinging them with things that are valuable to them, content that helps them with whatever problem they're trying to solve, which is what you've done a great job of teaching your customers with a Storybrand is to really identify what that is. And then the nurturing campaign allows you to feed that by providing content that helps them to grow. You say educate. So what do you mean? Like educate them about, do you mean start solving some of their problems as it relates to their products and services or educate them on why they need your product or what do you mean by educating? All the above. One of the mistakes that we make is that we feel like everything has to be gated, that everything has to be a pay to play. But the reality is people do business with companies they trust. And I don't know about you, I trust companies that are willing to give me something that aren't always asking for my credit card. So with this nurture campaign, this education, you really want to provide content that's valuable to them that they can take action on. Now you don't want to give everything away, but you want to tease. You want to say, here's what you can do. Here's a little piece. And if you want to take it further, you want to maximize it, you want to 10X it, then you buy my course. I've heard it said give away the why and charge for the how. So here's why you should be doing this, why, why, why. Now if you want me to show you how, you can charge for that. So that's one way if you want to think of it that way. All right, what does a typical nurture campaign look like? Great question. A typical nurture campaign is going to be four to five emails, maybe spread out over 10 to 15 days that drips content to them, that reminds them that you're there. The first email for instance. I would imagine it deepens the branding or deepens the impact. They've gone to your website, they've downloaded a PDF or gone to some sort of lead generator, maybe a Facebook ad. And for 15 days, you're just continuing to remind them, hey, I'm here, I'm here, I'm here, I'm here, I'm here. And providing great content for them. Yes. Building trust. And you know, for instance, might be the delivery of the asset that you said you were going to send to them. So it's an e-book or a worksheet or a list, a checklist or something. The second email a few days later might be a reminder to open it. It might be additional content. It might be ways to use that piece of material. Again, feeding them through that chain. Now the biggest key to nurturing is personalization. You really want to be able to respond in a way that they're comfortable with and that knows that you're paying attention. Have you ever gotten an email that says, hey, it's time to pay this bill? If you've already paid it, disregard this email. Nobody wants that. Nobody wants an email that says, hey, if you already downloaded this, we're not paying attention. They want to know that you know what's happened. And so if they have downloaded the asset, get them a different email that speaks to them having downloaded it. If they haven't downloaded it, send them an email to remind them to download it. This personalization is really, really important. You have to move away from a one-size-fits-all mentality. All right. So the first email was what again? Delivering the content. Delivering the content. Second, if they haven't opened it, you want to give them a... What about next? The third email is going to depend on what they do. But in general terms, the third email is going to be a new piece of educational content that plays off of the other. It's a little bit further in. So if you've got an eight-point checklist that you want to sell them into a course, you may give away the first two or three. But you don't want to overwhelm them. We know that people, they won't consume it all at once. And so you really want to drip it to them. So that third email is going to be some additional content, something that helps to deepen the relationship. Right. Okay. And then fourth, or do we keep going? Fourth or fifth, to me, the fourth or fifth email is really just going to be a restatement of the first couple of emails. Hey, notice you didn't get a chance to read this, or hey, I want to remind you that this great thing exists. It almost transitions into a sales campaign. Right. A sales campaign is really just a nurture campaign that has moved beyond the dating period to your point, the analogy you use of getting married. You've got to have a call to action. So that sales campaign is the tail end of a nurture campaign. And that's when you're saying, hey guys, it's time to buy. Right. It's time to buy. How many sales, now you've nurtured, nurtured, nurtured. How many sales emails, how strong do you want to be in those emails? You know, it depends on the type of product you have. It depends on the nature of your services. So if you're a real estate investor and you've got properties that you're trying to get people to look at, the emails have to come a little bit quicker. But if you're someone who's got a product that's really a standalone product, then you can space it out. Our rule of thumb at Infusionsoft always is you can email as often as you want. You can email as soon as you want as long as you're providing content. As long as you're providing valuable content. As long as you're buying valuable content. Once that value drops, they're going to opt out. Great. Really valuable for folks. We have a nurturing campaign that we basically just say every week, hey, the new podcast is out. Here's what we talk about in the new podcast. Here's some practical takeaways that you're going to get. And a lot of people open that, but a lot of people actually don't. And what I say to people, look, if 40% of your audience or even 30% of your audience is opening it and 60 or 70 is not, that's still fine because your brand is going across their eyeballs once a week. If they unsubscribe, you might be concerned about that, but they may just delete this without reading it because they don't think they need that that week, but they're not unsubscribing. Correct. And you're reminding them that you exist so that when that ping comes, when they need whatever it is you offer, you've emailed them within the last week and they think about you. Yeah, absolutely. And don't be afraid to go into a long-term nurture. What we've described as a short-term nurture, if they're not responding to your emails, they're not reading, but they haven't opted out, there's long-term nurtures, which maybe once a quarter or once a month that just maybe it's a video or maybe it's a quick hit. It's just a little piece of content to remind them you're there so that when they're ready, even if it bubbles back up again, they know where to go. Now, I've got a great example of this. There's a charter school down in Arizona that's called Legacy Traditional, and they're the largest public charter school in Arizona. And their director of marketing started with them about a year and a half ago. And when he got there, you told me a story about some of your clients that they don't do email marketing. And he walked in, he's like, all right, show me your email marketing campaigns. They're like, what's email marketing? They had no idea what email marketing was. And so after he did a face palm, he said, okay, let's get started. And he went to their home page, their enrollment page, and it was a very cumbersome form. It was four pages of content that they probably weren't going to have the answers to anyway. And he said, hold on, I'm going to do a very simple form that says, send me more information. Name, email address, two things. He sent a series of five emails over about a month. He spread it out a little bit because it was a little bit different buying cycle. And they added 1,300 additional enrollments just off that one form. And then we think about how big it is to transfer your kid to the school or to send your kid to the school. This is not run down to Walgreens and impulse buy on something. It's huge, but email is a way to on-ramp people to something really big, to a big purchase. We want to be creative about this, but you're right. It's value and it's content.
